phase.title: Wire context managers into ALL flow bodies
phase.description:
All 15 Prefect flow files import either flow_observability_context (CPU flows) or gpu_flow_observability_context (GPU flows) but none actually wrap their flow body in a `with` statement. This phase adds the one-line context manager invocation to every flow, giving all flows structured JSONL logging, flow_start/flow_end events, and (for GPU flows) CUDA guard + GPU heartbeat monitoring.
GPU flows (4): train_flow, hpo_flow, post_training_flow, analysis_flow CPU flows (11): acquisition_flow, data_flow, deploy_flow, dashboard_flow, biostatistics_flow, annotation_flow, drift_simulation_flow, synthetic_generation_flow, qa_flow, maintenance_flow, pipeline_flow

task.title: Wire gpu_flow_observability_context into 4 GPU flows
task.description:
For each GPU flow (train, hpo, post_training, analysis), wrap the main flow function body in:
with gpu_flow_observability_context( FLOW_NAME_TRAIN, logs_dir=Path(os.environ.get("LOGS_DIR", "/app/logs")), ) as event_logger: # ... existing flow body indented one level ...
The event_logger is available for downstream use (e.g., passing to trainer). Each GPU flow already imports gpu_flow_observability_context -- no new imports needed. The LOGS_DIR env var is already set in docker-compose.flows.yml x-common-env. The context manager calls require_cuda_context() and starts GpuHeartbeatMonitor automatically.
task.acceptance: Each GPU flow body is wrapped in gpu_flow_observability_context. Running the flow (in Docker with MINIVESS_ALLOW_HOST=1 for test) produces flow_start and flow_end events in LOGS_DIR/events.jsonl. GpuHeartbeatMonitor is active during the flow execution.

task.title: Wire flow_observability_context into 11 CPU flows
task.description:
For each CPU flow (acquisition, data, deploy, dashboard, biostatistics, annotation, drift_simulation, synthetic_generation, qa, maintenance, pipeline), wrap the main flow function body in:
with flow_observability_context( FLOW_NAME_BIOSTATISTICS, logs_dir=Path(os.environ.get("LOGS_DIR", "/app/logs")), ) as event_logger: # ... existing flow body indented one level ...
Each CPU flow already imports flow_observability_context. No CUDA guard or heartbeat -- just structured JSONL event logging and stdout flush.
task.acceptance: All 11 CPU flows produce flow_start/flow_end JSONL events when run. No flow body executes without the observability context wrapper.

task.title: AST-based enforcement test: every @flow body uses context manager
task.description:
Write a structural test that parses all 15 flow files with ast.parse() and verifies that every function decorated with @flow contains a `with` statement whose context expression references either flow_observability_context or gpu_flow_observability_context. This prevents regression -- any new flow added without the context manager fails the test.
Uses ast.walk() to find FunctionDef nodes with @flow decorator, then checks for ast.With nodes in the body whose context_expr calls the expected function. Per CLAUDE.md Rule #16: use ast.parse(), never regex.
task.acceptance: Test discovers all 15 flow files, finds all @flow-decorated functions, and asserts each contains a `with` context manager call. Test fails if any flow is missing the wrapper.

phase.title: Prefect task hooks on ALL @task decorators
phase.description:
The prefect_hooks.py module provides create_task_timing_hooks() which returns (on_completion, on_failure) callables compatible with Prefect 3.x @task hooks. Currently, ZERO @task decorators in any flow file use these hooks. There are approximately 70 @task-decorated functions across 15 flow files.
Strategy: define module-level hook tuple in each flow file, then add on_completion=[_on_complete] and on_failure=[_on_fail] to every @task decorator. This gives per-task timing and failure logging across the entire pipeline.

task.title: Add task timing hooks to all @task decorators
task.description:
In each flow file that contains @task decorators: 1. Add import: from minivess.observability.prefect_hooks import create_task_timing_hooks 2. Add module-level: _on_complete, _on_fail = create_task_timing_hooks() 3. Modify every @task(...) to include on_completion=[_on_complete], on_failure=[_on_fail]
Example transformation:
# Before: @task(name="train-one-fold") def train_one_fold_task(...):
# After: @task(name="train-one-fold", on_completion=[_on_complete], on_failure=[_on_fail]) def train_one_fold_task(...):
This affects all flow files with @task decorators: train_flow (~4 tasks), analysis_flow (~12 tasks), biostatistics_flow (~15 tasks), data_flow (~12 tasks), deploy_flow (~5 tasks), dashboard_flow (~5 tasks), hpo_flow (~1 task), acquisition_flow (~6 tasks), drift_simulation_flow (~3 tasks), annotation_flow (~2 tasks), post_training_flow (~1 task), qa_flow (~3 tasks), pipeline_flow (~1 task), maintenance_flow (~1 task), synthetic_generation_flow (~1 task).
task.acceptance: Every @task decorator across all 15 flow files includes on_completion and on_failure hook lists referencing create_task_timing_hooks() output.

task.title: AST-based enforcement test: every @task has hooks
task.description:
Write a structural test using ast.parse() that scans all flow files and verifies every @task decorator includes both on_completion and on_failure keyword arguments. This prevents regression when new tasks are added.
The test should: 1. Glob all *_flow.py files under src/minivess/orchestration/flows/ 2. Parse each with ast.parse() 3. Find all FunctionDef nodes decorated with @task 4. Assert each @task call has on_completion and on_failure keyword arguments 5. Report which task in which file is missing hooks
task.acceptance: Test discovers all ~70 @task decorators across 15 files and asserts each has both hook keyword arguments. Test fails if any @task is missing hooks.

phase.title: Trainer integration (epoch logging)
phase.description: The StructuredEventLogger has log_epoch_complete() which emits epoch_complete JSONL events with train_loss, val_loss, val_dice, lr, ETA, gpu_util, and vram_used. It also updates heartbeat.json for quick status checks. However, SegmentationTrainer never calls it. This phase threads the event_logger from the flow-level context manager into the trainer's epoch loop.

task.title: Thread StructuredEventLogger into SegmentationTrainer
task.description:
Modify SegmentationTrainer.__init__() to accept an optional event_logger parameter of type StructuredEventLogger | None. Store it as self._event_logger.
In the epoch completion path (after validation metrics are computed), call:
if self._event_logger is not None: self._event_logger.log_epoch_complete( epoch=epoch, max_epochs=self.config.max_epochs, train_loss=train_loss, val_loss=val_loss, val_dice=val_dice, lr=current_lr, epoch_wall_s=epoch_duration, gpu_util_pct=gpu_snapshot.get("gpu_util_pct", 0), vram_used_mb=gpu_snapshot.get("gpu_memory_used_mb", 0), )
The gpu_snapshot comes from the existing gpu_heartbeat module's _get_gpu_snapshot() function (graceful degradation if no GPU).
Also call log_training_start() at the beginning of the training loop and log_training_end() after all folds complete.
task.acceptance: When SegmentationTrainer is constructed with an event_logger, each epoch completion produces an epoch_complete event in events.jsonl. heartbeat.json is updated with current epoch, loss, dice, and ETA. training_start and training_end events bracket the training loop.

task.title: Pass event_logger from train_flow to SegmentationTrainer
task.description:
In train_flow.py, the gpu_flow_observability_context yields an event_logger (StructuredEventLogger). Pass this event_logger to SegmentationTrainer when constructing it inside the flow body:
with gpu_flow_observability_context(...) as event_logger: trainer = SegmentationTrainer( model=model, config=config, event_logger=event_logger, # NEW ... )
Similarly wire event_logger into hpo_flow.py where it constructs trainers for HPO trials. post_training_flow and analysis_flow do not construct trainers directly, so they do not need this wiring.
task.acceptance: Running train_flow produces epoch-level JSONL events alongside flow-level events. The events.jsonl file contains training_start, epoch_complete (per epoch), and training_end events interleaved with flow_start/flow_end events.

phase.title: Docker Compose modifications (HEALTHCHECK, LGTM, DCGM, OTEL)
phase.description: The infrastructure docker-compose.yml has OTel collector, Prometheus, and Grafana but the flow docker-compose.flows.yml has no HEALTHCHECK directives on any flow service. Additionally, there is no DCGM (NVIDIA Data Center GPU Manager) exporter for hardware-level GPU metrics, and the OTel collector is not connected to the flow containers via environment variables. This phase adds these missing pieces.

task.title: Add HEALTHCHECK to all flow services in docker-compose.flows.yml
task.description:
Add Docker HEALTHCHECK directives to each flow service in docker-compose.flows.yml. The base Dockerfiles already have a HEALTHCHECK that verifies the Python interpreter, but per-flow health checks should be more specific.
For long-running services (dashboard-api, dashboard-ui, monai-label):
healthcheck: test: ["CMD-SHELL", "curl -sf http://localhost:${PORT}/health || exit 1"] interval: 30s timeout: 10s retries: 3 start_period: 60s
For batch flow services (train, data, biostatistics, etc.) that run to completion and exit, use a heartbeat file check:
healthcheck: test: ["CMD-SHELL", "test -f /app/logs/heartbeat.json && python3 -c \"import json,sys,time; h=json.load(open('/app/logs/heartbeat.json')); age=time.time()-__import__('datetime').datetime.fromisoformat(h['timestamp']).timestamp(); sys.exit(0 if age<300 else 1)\""] interval: 60s timeout: 10s retries: 5 start_period: 120s
This leverages the heartbeat.json file that StructuredEventLogger.update_heartbeat() writes. A heartbeat older than 300 seconds indicates a stalled flow.
task.acceptance: Every service in docker-compose.flows.yml has a healthcheck directive. The test parses the YAML and asserts all services have healthcheck keys with test, interval, timeout, retries, and start_period fields.

task.title: Add DCGM exporter and wire OTel endpoint into flow containers
task.description:
Two related infrastructure additions that connect flow containers to the monitoring stack:
A) Add NVIDIA DCGM exporter as a service in the monitoring profile of docker-compose.yml. DCGM provides hardware-level GPU metrics (temperature, power draw, ECC errors, NVLink throughput, PCIe throughput) that pynvml-based heartbeat monitoring cannot capture.
dcgm-exporter: image: nvcr.io/nvidia/k8s/dcgm-exporter:3.3.8-3.6.1-ubuntu22.04 container_name: minivess-dcgm-exporter profiles: ["monitoring", "full"] devices: - "nvidia.com/gpu=all" ports: - "${DCGM_PORT:-9400}:9400" restart: unless-stopped
Also add a Prometheus scrape target in deployment/prometheus/prometheus.yml for dcgm-exporter:9400/metrics.
B) Add OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT to x-common-env in docker-compose.flows.yml so that all flow containers can send traces to the OTel collector:
OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT: ${OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT:-http://minivess-otel-collector:4317} OTEL_SERVICE_NAME: ${FLOW_NAME:-minivess-unknown}
This enables TelemetryProvider (telemetry.py) to connect to the OTel collector without any code changes -- it reads the endpoint from environment. The OTEL_SERVICE_NAME is set per-flow via the existing FLOW_NAME env var.
Add DCGM_PORT and OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT to .env.example.
task.acceptance: docker-compose.yml monitoring profile includes dcgm-exporter service. Prometheus config includes dcgm-exporter scrape target. x-common-env in docker-compose.flows.yml includes OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT and OTEL_SERVICE_NAME. All flow containers inherit these via YAML anchor. DCGM_PORT and OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT are in .env.example.

task.title: Add Grafana LGTM stack service definitions
task.description:
Add Loki (log aggregation), Tempo (distributed tracing), and Mimir (long-term metrics) to docker-compose.yml under the monitoring profile. These complement the existing Prometheus + Grafana services to form the full LGTM stack.
Loki receives structured logs from flow containers via the Docker log driver (or a Promtail sidecar). Tempo receives OTel trace spans from the OTel collector (already configured). Mimir provides long-term metric storage behind Prometheus remote_write.
loki: image: grafana/loki:3.4.2 container_name: minivess-loki profiles: ["monitoring", "full"] ports: - "${LOKI_PORT:-3100}:3100" volumes: - loki_data:/loki command: -config.file=/etc/loki/local-config.yaml restart: unless-stopped
tempo: image: grafana/tempo:2.7.2 container_name: minivess-tempo profiles: ["monitoring", "full"] ports: - "${TEMPO_PORT:-3200}:3200" volumes: - tempo_data:/tmp/tempo command: -config.file=/etc/tempo/tempo.yaml restart: unless-stopped
Add Grafana datasource provisioning for Loki and Tempo in deployment/grafana/provisioning/datasources/.
NOTE: Mimir is deferred to a future pass -- Prometheus local storage is sufficient for the current scale (<100 training runs).
task.acceptance: docker-compose.yml monitoring profile includes loki and tempo services. Grafana datasource provisioning files exist for both. OTel collector config (otel-collector-config.yml) exports traces to Tempo endpoint. LOKI_PORT and TEMPO_PORT are in .env.example.

phase.title: Cross-wiring (stall detection, OTel dependency)
phase.description:
Two standalone observability modules need cross-wiring into the active pipeline: 1. stall_detection.py has detect_mlflow_metric_stall() but nothing calls it during flow execution or monitoring checks. 2. TelemetryProvider (telemetry.py) creates OTel spans but is never instantiated inside flow context managers -- flows cannot emit distributed traces.
This phase connects both into the existing observability infrastructure.

task.title: Integrate stall detection into dashboard health checks
task.description:
The dashboard_flow.py includes QA health checks (merged from qa_flow.py per PR #567). Add stall detection as an additional health check that runs during the dashboard flow:
1. Import detect_mlflow_metric_stall from stall_detection.py 2. Add a new @task "check-training-stall" that discovers active MLflow runs (status == RUNNING) and calls detect_mlflow_metric_stall() for each 3. Wire the task into the dashboard flow's QA section 4. Emit Prometheus alert metric if any run is stale
Also integrate stall detection into the GpuHeartbeatMonitor as an optional cross-check: if the heartbeat thread detects low GPU utilization AND MLflow metrics are stale, emit a combined diagnostic event to events.jsonl with both signals.
task.acceptance: Dashboard flow health check section includes stall detection for active runs. GpuHeartbeatMonitor optionally cross-references MLflow metric staleness with low GPU utilization. Combined diagnostic events appear in events.jsonl when both conditions are met.

task.title: Wire TelemetryProvider into flow observability context
task.description:
Modify flow_observability_context and gpu_flow_observability_context in flow_observability.py to optionally create a TelemetryProvider and wrap the flow body in an OTel span:
1. Check if OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT is set in the environment 2. If set, instantiate TelemetryProvider with the endpoint 3. Wrap the flow body in a span named "flow/{flow_name}" 4. Add flow_name, status, and duration as span attributes 5. If OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT is not set, skip OTel (graceful degradation)
This means flows automatically emit OTel traces when the LGTM stack is active (docker compose --profile monitoring) and silently skip when it is not (local dev). No code changes needed in individual flow files.
task.acceptance: When OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT is set, flow context managers create OTel spans with flow name, status, and duration attributes. When not set, no OTel code is executed (no import errors, no connection attempts). The opentelemetry packages are optional dependencies -- ImportError is caught gracefully.

task.title: Add OTel span context to Prefect task hooks
task.description:
Extend create_task_timing_hooks() in prefect_hooks.py to optionally create child OTel spans for each task:
1. Accept an optional TelemetryProvider parameter 2. If provided, each on_completion/on_failure hook creates a child span with task_name, duration, and status attributes 3. If not provided, hooks behave as before (logger only)
Wire this through the flow context managers: when OTel is active, pass the TelemetryProvider to create_task_timing_hooks() so that task spans nest under the parent flow span.
task.acceptance: When OTel is active, each @task execution produces a child span under the flow span. Span hierarchy: flow/train -> task/train-one-fold -> task/log-fold-results. When OTel is inactive, no spans are created and hooks log to Python logger only.

phase.title: Verification (staging tests, docker demo, behavioral tests)
phase.description: Final verification phase ensuring all observability wiring from Phases 1-5 works end-to-end. Includes staging-tier tests (no model loading, no Docker), a Docker Compose integration demo script, and behavioral tests that verify the complete observability pipeline produces the expected artifacts.

task.title: Staging-tier test suite for all observability wiring
task.description:
Run make test-staging and verify: 1. All new enforcement tests pass (flow context wiring, task hooks) 2. All existing observability tests still pass 3. Zero new test skips introduced 4. Zero ruff or mypy errors in modified files
Fix any failures discovered. Per CLAUDE.md Rule #23: gather ALL failures first (--maxfail=200), categorize by root cause, fix in batches.
task.acceptance: make test-staging passes with 0 failures, 0 new skips. ruff check and mypy report no errors in modified files.

task.title: Docker Compose integration demo
task.description:
Create a demo script (scripts/demo_observability.sh) that: 1. Starts the monitoring profile: docker compose --profile monitoring up -d 2. Runs the biostatistics flow (CPU, lightweight, fast) 3. Verifies events.jsonl contains flow_start and flow_end events 4. Verifies heartbeat.json exists and has recent timestamp 5. Checks Grafana datasources include Loki and Tempo 6. Checks Prometheus targets include dcgm-exporter (if GPU available) 7. Tears down the stack
This script is a manual verification tool, not a CI test. It validates the Docker Compose wiring end-to-end in the real container environment.

tests.test: Manual: run scripts/demo_observability.sh and verify output

task.acceptance: Script completes without errors on a machine with Docker and the monitoring profile services. events.jsonl and heartbeat.json contain expected data. Grafana is reachable and shows Loki/Tempo datasources.

task.title: Behavioral tests: end-to-end observability artifact verification
task.description:
Write behavioral tests that verify the full observability pipeline produces correct artifacts. These tests use MINIVESS_ALLOW_HOST=1 escape hatch (no Docker required) and mock MLflow:
1. test_flow_produces_jsonl_events: Run a minimal flow variant and assert events.jsonl contains flow_start, task_end (for each task), and flow_end events in chronological order. 2. test_gpu_flow_writes_heartbeat: Run a GPU flow variant (mocked CUDA) and assert heartbeat.json is written with expected keys. 3. test_epoch_events_in_training: Run SegmentationTrainer with event_logger and assert epoch_complete events appear with correct fields (epoch, max_epochs, train_loss, val_loss, val_dice, lr, epoch_wall_s, eta_s). 4. test_stale_run_detected: Create a mock MLflow run with old timestamps and assert detect_mlflow_metric_stall() returns stale=True. 5. test_otel_graceful_degradation: Unset OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT and verify flow completes without OTel errors.
task.acceptance: All 5 behavioral tests pass in staging tier. No model loading, no Docker, no cloud credentials required. Tests verify the shape and content of observability artifacts, not just that code runs without errors.

key_risks.risk: Phase 1 indentation changes in 15 flow files may cause merge conflicts with concurrent feature branches. Recommend merging early.
DCGM exporter (Phase 4.2) requires NVIDIA GPU on the Docker host. Graceful skip when GPU is absent, but Prometheus scrape target will show as DOWN.
OTel dependency (Phase 5) adds opentelemetry-* packages. These are already optional extras in pyproject.toml. ImportError is handled gracefully.
